title: The Private Health Fund Cliff That Allied Health Practices Hit Every July
excerpt: Allied health practices across Queensland see their strongest June bookings as private health fund extras approach their annual limit. Then July arrives, bookings soften, and costs hold firm. Planning for this pattern starts now.
content:
June is consistently one of the strongest revenue months for allied health practices across Queensland, NSW, and Victoria. Physiotherapy clinics, psychology practices, podiatry, occupational therapy, and speech pathology businesses see their appointment books fill in the final weeks of the financial year as patients with remaining private health fund extras benefits look to use their allocation before it lapses.

The July picture is different. Appointment volumes typically soften after June 30, sometimes materially, as patients who exhausted their annual extras allocation return to market more slowly. The clinical team that was fully booked through June carries sessions below full utilisation, the fixed cost base stays the same, and the strong June collections in the account are funding the quieter month ahead.

This cycle is not a surprise to any practice principal who has managed a full calendar year. What separates well-prepared practices from reactive ones is whether the June profitability is used to plan for July before July arrives, rather than responding to the dip after it opens.

How Private Health Fund Extras Create the June Surge

Most private health fund extras policies run on a calendar year basis, with annual benefit limits resetting on 1 January. A patient with physiotherapy or psychology cover who has used their benefit steadily through the year arrives at May and June with a smaller remaining entitlement and a practical awareness that any unused amount disappears at year end. For many patients, this drives appointments that might otherwise be deferred.

The session that felt discretionary in March becomes a deliberate booking in June when the alternative is leaving an entitlement unused. For practices with active recall systems and proactive patient communication around benefit periods, June appointment volumes can exceed any other month in the year. The bookings are real clinical encounters, but their timing is shaped by fund cycle mechanics rather than by the natural rhythm of patient demand.

Medicare rebate cycling adds a secondary factor for psychology practices. Patients who have worked through their standard GP Mental Health Care Plan sessions earlier in the year may seek final sessions before June 30 under existing referrals. These effects compound the private health fund dynamic and concentrate appointments into the same six-week window.

What July Does to the Cost Base

The challenge for allied health principals is that the fixed cost base does not move with appointment volumes in the short term. Clinical staff rostered on June's pattern are still employed and scheduled in July. Rent continues. Practice management software and equipment leases continue. The wage cost of the minimum clinical complement runs regardless of how full the appointment book is on any given day.

In allied health, the primary margin driver is the billable ratio: the share of each clinician's paid hours that converts to billable patient appointments. In a full June, experienced clinicians typically carry a strong ratio. When the appointment book softens by 15 to 20 percent in July, the same roster generates a lower ratio and the gap between paid hours and billable hours lands directly in the cost base without revenue to absorb it.

The Health Professionals and Support Services Award rolls to its updated rates from the first full pay period of July. The wage cost increase applies immediately across every eligible clinician while appointment volumes are still recovering from the June peak. July's P&L often looks noticeably weaker than the practice's average margin performance suggests it should, without any underlying decline in clinical quality or patient relationships.

What the June Accounts Make Possible to Plan

The twelve months of data available at EOFY create a clear picture of the practice's real margin structure: appointment volumes by modality, billable ratios by clinician, and the private health fund versus Medicare versus private-pay revenue split. This picture is most valuable when it is assembled before July begins rather than after the dip has already arrived.

A Workforce Capacity and Utilisation Review applied to an allied health practice identifies which clinicians carry the strongest billable ratios, which modalities hold their margin through quieter months, and where scheduling decisions can manage the July cost base rather than absorbing the dip passively. For practices with mixed modalities, understanding which service streams hold appointment stability through July and August directly informs the roster structure for the new financial year.

A 13-week cash flow forecast extended through September maps the appointment volume dip against committed outflows: the award wage increase from the first full pay period of July, superannuation for the April to June quarter due by 28 July, and any rent reviews effective from the start of the new financial year. The forward picture often looks more manageable once it is built than when the pressure arrives unplanned.

For practice principals thinking about a future sale or succession, the EOFY accounts are the foundation of the valuation conversation. Allied health practices where the principal holds the referring relationships personally, or where clinical delivery depends on the owner's direct involvement, attract significantly lower valuation multiples than practices with a capable team that holds patient relationships independently. An Exit Readiness Diagnostic at this point scores the practice across the buyer-grade readiness dimensions and identifies where the structural effort would most move the eventual sale outcome.

If June has been strong and the July and August position has not yet been mapped against what is coming, that forward view is worth building before the financial year closes. faqs.q: Why do physiotherapy and allied health practices get so busy in June in Australia?
faqs.a: Most private health fund extras policies reset on 1 January, which means patients who have used part of their annual physiotherapy, psychology, or podiatry benefit arrive at May and June with a remaining allocation they are motivated to use before it lapses. Combined with Medicare cycling for GP Mental Health Care Plan sessions, the last six weeks of the financial year concentrate demand in a way that creates June appointment volumes above the practice's average. The pattern is predictable and consistent across the sector each year.

faqs.q: What is billable ratio for an allied health practice and why does it affect profit?
faqs.a: Billable ratio is the proportion of each clinician's paid hours that generates a billed patient appointment. It is the central margin driver in allied health because the largest cost, clinical wages, runs whether appointments are filled or not. When June's full appointment book gives way to a quieter July, the ratio falls and the wage cost base absorbs the gap without corresponding revenue to offset it. Tracking this monthly by clinician and modality identifies where scheduling and recall systems have the most immediate impact on profitability.

faqs.q: How does the HPSS Award wage increase affect allied health practices from July in Australia?
faqs.a: The Health Professionals and Support Services Award sets minimum rates for most allied health clinical and support staff. Updated rates take effect from the first full pay period of July, applying immediately across every eligible role while appointment volumes are typically still recovering from the June peak. The compound effect of a higher wage base against a softer appointment book compresses the practice's margin in the opening weeks of the new financial year. Modelling the full payroll impact before the new rates land allows cash flow planning to reflect the actual July cost base.

faqs.q: How can a physiotherapy or allied health practice plan for the post-June appointment slowdown?
faqs.a: Three practical steps make the most difference before June 30. Build a forward cash flow view through September that maps the expected appointment reduction against committed outflows including the HPSS Award increase and superannuation due by 28 July. Review billable ratios by clinician and modality using the full year's data to understand which service streams hold their margin most stably in quieter months. And consider proactive June recalls to build August bookings alongside June's surge rather than letting the July book start from a flat base. A 13-week cash flow forecast built for an allied health practice incorporates the appointment cycle timing specifically.

faqs.q: What financial metrics should a physiotherapy or allied health practice track monthly?
faqs.a: The most useful metrics are billable ratio by clinician, revenue per clinical hour by modality, the private health fund versus Medicare versus private-pay revenue split, debtor days on outstanding invoices, and clinical staff cost as a percentage of total revenue. Together these tell a practice principal whether clinical capacity is converting efficiently to revenue and whether the revenue mix is shifting in ways that affect margin. Most practice management software holds the underlying data; the question is whether it is being reviewed on a structured monthly cadence rather than only at EOFY.

faqs.q: What is a typical valuation multiple for an allied health practice in Australia?
faqs.a: Allied health practices typically attract two to four times normalised annual profit where the principal is the primary referring clinician and patient relationships sit with the owner personally. Practices with a capable clinical team, independent patient relationships across multiple practitioners, and stable revenue across the appointment cycle can attract four to six times or higher. The discount for principal dependence is consistently one of the largest factors compressing allied health valuations. Business valuations for Queensland SMEs covers allied health and medical practices as part of the healthcare cluster.

faqs.q: When should an allied health practice owner in Brisbane start preparing for sale or succession?
faqs.a: Starting three to five years before an intended exit gives the practice time to address the structural factors most consistently suppressing allied health valuations: principal dependence in patient referrals, single-modality concentration risk, and the absence of independently functioning clinical team members. An Exit Readiness Diagnostic done at EOFY, when twelve months of appointment and margin data are available, establishes the baseline and identifies which of the eight buyer-grade readiness dimensions needs the most focused attention in the years before a planned transition.
